{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 1.0,
    "R1.4": 0.0,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 0.7,
    "R1.7": 0.0,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.6", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Making a paper 'stronger' centrally involves sharpening the thesis and contribution claim; the current framing of role-specialization as the load-bearing contribution is asserted rather than argued.",
    "R1.2": "Logical consistency is severely compromised: the text claims BioCrew 'substantially reduces both error modes' and that 9.6% FPR is 'a meaningful improvement over 7.2%' — but 9.6 > 7.2 and 13.4 > 11.8, so BioCrew is strictly worse on both metrics. Whether to fix this logical reversal is plausibly central to the task.",
    "R1.3": "Evidence-claim alignment is the most acute problem in the artifact. Every top-level empirical claim contradicts the reported numbers. This dimension is load-bearing regardless of which interpretation of 'stronger' is chosen, because polishing prose that misrepresents data would deepen the problem.",
    "R1.6": "Writing polish is a natural and common reading of 'make this stronger'; the text is also repetitive (role-specialization is restated nearly verbatim four times), which polish would address."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Should I strengthen this by improving prose quality and rhetorical framing while preserving the current empirical conclusions — or by identifying and resolving logical and evidential inconsistencies, including the apparent reversal where BioCrew's reported error rates (FPR 9.6%, FNR 13.4%) are both higher than the baseline (FPR 7.2%, FNR 11.8%), yet the text claims substantial improvement on both?"
}

rationale.R1.1: Making a paper 'stronger' centrally involves sharpening the thesis and contribution claim; the current framing of role-specialization as the load-bearing contribution is asserted rather than argued.
rationale.R1.2: Logical consistency is severely compromised: the text claims BioCrew 'substantially reduces both error modes' and that 9.6% FPR is 'a meaningful improvement over 7.2%' — but 9.6 > 7.2 and 13.4 > 11.8, so BioCrew is strictly worse on both metrics. Whether to fix this logical reversal is plausibly central to the task.
rationale.R1.3: Evidence-claim alignment is the most acute problem in the artifact. Every top-level empirical claim contradicts the reported numbers. This dimension is load-bearing regardless of which interpretation of 'stronger' is chosen, because polishing prose that misrepresents data would deepen the problem.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is a natural and common reading of 'make this stronger'; the text is also repetitive (role-specialization is restated nearly verbatim four times), which polish would address.

parsed.clarification_question: Should I strengthen this by improving prose quality and rhetorical framing while preserving the current empirical conclusions — or by identifying and resolving logical and evidential inconsistencies, including the apparent reversal where BioCrew's reported error rates (FPR 9.6%, FNR 13.4%) are both higher than the baseline (FPR 7.2%, FNR 11.8%), yet the text claims substantial improvement on both?
